---
title: "Methods in R"
subtitle: "A topic reference for biomedical research and peer review"
author: "R. Heller"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: bookdown::bookdown_site
documentclass: book
bibliography: [references.bib, packages.bib]
biblio-style: apalike
csl: style/vancouver.csl
link-citations: yes
github-repo: r-heller/methods-in-r
url: https://r-heller.github.io/methods-in-r/
description: "A bench reference for biomedical authors and peer reviewers — every common method covered with R code, diagnostics, and reviewer-facing red flags."
cover-image: "images/cover.png"
---

# Preface {-}

```{r cover, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, out.width = "60%", fig.align = "center", fig.alt = "Methods in R book cover"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/cover.png")
```

*A topic reference for biomedical research and peer review.*

## Why this book exists {-}

The R/biostatistics shelf is crowded — Moscarelli's *Biostatistics With 'R'*,
Srinivasan's *R for Basic Biostatistics in Medical Research*, Harrell's
*Biostatistics for Biomedical Research*, Shahbaba's *Biostatistics with R*,
the Cambridge text, the Springer epidemiology volume, and Lepš & Šmilauer
on the ecological side. They are good books. They are also linear
textbooks: open at page one, work to the end.

This book is shaped differently, around two readers it expects to serve
in the same week:

1. **The author** who has data in front of them and needs to pick a
   method, run it correctly in R, and write the Methods paragraph.
2. **The peer reviewer** who has a manuscript on their desk and needs
   to know — for the method the authors used — what a competent
   application looks like, which diagnostics should have been reported,
   and what red flags to flag.

Every method page therefore ends with a **For Reviewers** section: the
misapplications, the missing diagnostics, the red flags in tables and
figures, and the things to verify against the authors' references and
arithmetic. The book opens at Chapter \@ref(sec-find-your-method), a
decision tree from research question to method, not at a glossy
introduction. A final part, *Reporting Templates*, gives copy-paste
Methods + Results paragraphs tagged with
**STROBE / CONSORT / TRIPOD-AI / PRISMA / ARRIVE**.

## Provenance and attribution {-}

The substantive content is consolidated and re-organised from two of my
earlier public, MIT-licensed repositories that served as inspiration:

- **`CTTIR/courses`** — <https://github.com/CTTIR/courses>. A set of
  hands-on lab materials. The labs have been re-homed by topic in this
  book rather than reproduced in their original sequence.
- **`CTTIR/tutorials`** — <https://github.com/CTTIR/tutorials>. A
  collection of method pages written to a nine-section template. The
  template is preserved and extended here with a *For Reviewers* tenth
  section.

Two pieces of original material are added on top:

- The **For Reviewers** sections, written specifically for this volume.
- The **Reporting Templates** part, which aggregates Methods + Results
  paragraphs by reporting guideline.

A decision-tree wizard remains live at
<https://cttir.github.io/tutorials/decision-tree/> — Chapter 0 mirrors
the static tree and links out to the wizard for clickable navigation.

## Sibling volume {-}

This is the second volume in the *…in R* series. The first, on a
different topic, is **`r-heller/strategy-in-r`** — *Strategy in R: Game
Theory, Simulation, and Machine Intelligence*. The two share branding,
build conventions (bookdown, `renv`, GitHub Pages deploy), and a
preference for R-only worked examples over toy abstractions.

## How to read {-}

If you already know the method, jump to its chapter. If not, start at
Chapter \@ref(sec-find-your-method). If you are reviewing a paper, search
for the method by name and read the *For Reviewers* section first.

## Licence {-}

Code under MIT. Prose under CC BY 4.0 with attribution to *R. Heller,
Methods in R*.

```{r packages-bib, include = FALSE, eval = TRUE}
knitr::write_bib(c(.packages(), "bookdown", "knitr", "rmarkdown"),
                 "packages.bib")
```
